questions.question: Define natural numbers and whole numbers with examples.
questions.answer: Natural numbers are counting numbers starting from 1 such as 1, 2, 3, 4. Whole numbers include all natural numbers along with zero. Example: 0, 1, 2, 3.

questions.question: Explain integers with examples.
questions.answer: Integers are numbers that include positive numbers, negative numbers and zero. Examples include -3, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3.

questions.question: Define rational numbers and give examples.
questions.answer: A rational number is a number that can be expressed in the form p/q where p and q are integers and q ≠ 0. Examples include 1/2, 3/4 and 5.

questions.question: Define irrational numbers and give examples.
questions.answer: Irrational numbers are numbers that cannot be written in the form p/q. Their decimal expansion is non-terminating and non-repeating. Examples include √2, √3 and π.

questions.question: Explain real numbers with examples.
questions.answer: Real numbers include all rational and irrational numbers. They represent all numbers that can be shown on the number line.

questions.question: Explain square roots with examples.
questions.answer: The square root of a number is a value that when multiplied by itself gives the original number. Example: √9 = 3 and √16 = 4.

questions.question: Explain terminating decimals with examples.
questions.answer: A terminating decimal is a decimal number that ends after a finite number of digits. Example: 0.25 and 0.5.

questions.question: Explain recurring decimals with examples.
questions.answer: Recurring decimals are decimals in which digits repeat infinitely. Example: 0.333… and 0.666….

questions.question: Explain why √4 is rational.
questions.answer: √4 = 2, which is an integer. Since integers can be written in the form p/q, √4 is rational.

questions.question: Explain why √9 is rational.
questions.answer: √9 = 3. Since 3 is an integer, it can be written as 3/1 and is therefore rational.

questions.question: Explain why √5 is irrational.
questions.answer: √5 cannot be written in the form p/q and its decimal expansion is non-terminating and non-repeating.

questions.question: Explain why π is irrational.
questions.answer: π has a non-terminating and non-repeating decimal expansion and cannot be written as a fraction.

questions.question: Explain the concept of number line.
questions.answer: A number line is a straight line on which numbers are represented as points. Positive numbers lie on the right side of zero and negative numbers lie on the left side.

questions.question: Explain the classification of real numbers.
questions.answer: Real numbers are classified into rational numbers and irrational numbers.

questions.question: Explain why 0 is a rational number.
questions.answer: 0 can be written as 0/1 where numerator and denominator are integers.

questions.question: Explain why 1 is neither prime nor composite.
questions.answer: 1 has only one factor whereas prime numbers have two factors and composite numbers have more than two.

questions.question: Explain the meaning of prime numbers with examples.
questions.answer: Prime numbers are numbers that have exactly two factors: 1 and itself. Examples: 2, 3, 5.

questions.question: Explain composite numbers with examples.
questions.answer: Composite numbers are numbers that have more than two factors. Examples: 4, 6, 8.

questions.question: Explain coprime numbers with examples.
questions.answer: Two numbers are coprime if their HCF is 1. Example: 8 and 15.

questions.question: Explain HCF with an example.
questions.answer: HCF is the greatest number that divides two numbers exactly. Example: HCF of 12 and 18 is 6.

questions.question: Explain LCM with an example.
questions.answer: LCM is the smallest number divisible by two numbers. Example: LCM of 4 and 6 is 12.

questions.question: Explain prime factorisation with example.
questions.answer: Prime factorisation is expressing a number as product of prime numbers. Example: 60 = 2² × 3 × 5.

questions.question: Explain why every integer is rational.
questions.answer: Any integer can be written in the form p/q such as 5 = 5/1.

questions.question: Explain the decimal expansion of rational numbers.
questions.answer: The decimal expansion of rational numbers either terminates or repeats infinitely.

questions.question: Explain the importance of irrational numbers.
questions.answer: Irrational numbers help represent values that cannot be expressed as fractions and appear frequently in geometry such as √2 and π.
